coredata.dc:title: Analysis of synergistic effect between graphene and octahedral cuprous oxide in cuprous oxide-graphene composites and their photocatalytic application
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wei
Ma, Yujie
Yang, Zhi
Tang, Xuehui
Li, Xiaolin
He, Guili
Cheng, Yuqiao
Fang, Zebo
He, Rong
Zhang, Yafei
coredata.dc:description: The octahedral cuprous oxide (Cu2O) supported by reduced graphene oxide composites (Cu2O-RGO) are compounded via a gentle one-pot in situ method. The reduction of GO and the growth of octahedral Cu2O particles simultaneously occur with the Cu2O particles depositing on RGO nanosheets uniformly. The enhanced photocatalytic properties are appraised by decomposition of methyl orange under visible light irradiation. Compared with the pure octahedral Cu2O particles and RGO, the as-prepared composites degrade methyl orange completely within 50 min with apparent rate constant at 82.88 × 10−3 min−1, ten times of the pure octahedral Cu2O particles and twenty-seven times of RGO. Scavengers are also introduced to investigate the photocatalytic mechanism which turns out that h+ and O2 ¯ radicals are the main active species in the photocatalysis process. The synergistic effect between RGO and octahedral Cu2O particles in the photocatalysis process is systematacially analyzed for the first time. Carrier generation occurs on the Cu2O particle, and electron is collected and transported by graphene here to prolong the charge carriers' lifetime, leading to strong absorbance in visible light region, instant carrier separation, and fast charge transfer. Thus the efficiency of visible-light-driven photocatalysis is greatly enhanced.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Octahedral cuprous oxide-graphene
One-pot synthesis
Photocatalytic performance
Synergistic effect
Photocatalytic mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synergistic effect between RGO and octahedral Cu2O particles
Photocatalytic mechanisms of octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis of octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites.
SEM images of octahedral Cu2O particles (a and b) and Cu2O-RGO composites (c and d).
TEM and HRTEM images of octahedral Cu2O particles (a and b) and Cu2O-RGO composites (c and d).

(a) XRD patterns of GO, Cu2O-RGO and Cu2O. (b) The enlarged region in Fig. 4a which is emphasized by blue dotted line. (c) FT-IR spectra of GO, Cu2O-RGO and Cu2O. (d) Raman spectra of GO and Cu2O-RGO. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) The whole XPS spectrum of Cu2O-RGO; XPS spectra of (b) Cu 2p and (c) C 1s in Cu2O-RGO; (d) XPS spectrum of C 1s in GO.

(a) UV–Vis spectra of Cu2O and Cu2O-RGO. (b) (αhν)
2 as a function of photo energy of Cu2O and Cu2O-RGO. (c) PL spectra of Cu2O and Cu2O-RGO composites. (d) EIS of Cu2O and Cu2O-RGO at an open circuit between 0.01 Hz and 100 kHz. The inset is enlarged to show the solution resistances.
Schematic showing charge separation and transfer at the interface of octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites under visible light.
Comparison of degradability for graphene-based photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Analysis of synergistic effect between graphene and octahedral cuprous oxide in cuprous oxide-graphene composites and their photocatalytic application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Thin Film and Microfabrication of Ministry of Education, Department of Micro/Nano Electronics, School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai 200240, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Thin Film and Microfabrication of Ministry of Education
School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Shaoxing University, Shaoxing 312000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The octahedral cuprous oxide (Cu2O) supported by reduced graphene oxide composites (Cu2O-RGO) are compounded via a gentle one-pot in situ method. The reduction of GO and the growth of octahedral Cu2O particles simultaneously occur with the Cu2O particles depositing on RGO nanosheets uniformly. The enhanced photocatalytic properties are appraised by decomposition of methyl orange under visible light irradiation. Compared with the pure octahedral Cu2O particles and RGO, the as-prepared composites degrade methyl orange completely within 50 min with apparent rate constant at 82.88 × 10−3 min−1, ten times of the pure octahedral Cu2O particles and twenty-seven times of RGO. Scavengers are also introduced to investigate the photocatalytic mechanism which turns out that h+ and O2 ¯ radicals are the main active species in the photocatalysis process. The synergistic effect between RGO and octahedral Cu2O particles in the photocatalysis process is systematacially analyzed for the first time. Carrier generation occurs on the Cu2O particle, and electron is collected and transported by graphene here to prolong the charge carriers' lifetime, leading to strong absorbance in visible light region, instant carrier separation, and fast charge transfer. Thus the efficiency of visible-light-driven photocatalysis is greatly enhanced.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor-based photocatalysis, which owns extensive applications especially in effective use of solar energy and efficient environmental decontamination, has attracted a lot of scholars to study [1–5]. As p-type semiconductor (band gap ∼ 2.17 eV), with its advantages of abundance, low toxicity, and environmental compatibility, cuprous oxide (Cu2O) is regarded as a promising catalyst in the visible light degradation of organic pollutants [6–9]. Various shapes of Cu2O microcrystal have been reported to be prepared by modifying the synthesis methods and procedures, and the photocatalytic property was closely related with the exposed crystal faces [10–14]. Octahedral Cu2O microcrystals which possess whole (111) surfaces were studied to have a better photocatalytic efficiency in decomposing Rhodamine B than microcrystals of other shapes incorporating partial (100) and (110) surfaces [15]. The catalytic reduction of Cr (VI) under visible light irradiation by different shapes of Cu2O nanocrystals was also investigated, which turned out that photocatalytic specific activity over the Cu2O nanocrystals dominated by different facets followed the order {111}>{110}>{100} [16]. Thus octahedral Cu2O crystals with whole {111} facets are believed to have better photocatalytic performances.

Recently, carbon nanostructures such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots are promising materials in consisting of nanocomposites which perform well in catalysis field [17–19]. With great surface area, high mobility of charge carriers, and unique two-dimensional layer structure of sp
2 -hybridized carbon atoms, graphene has been an admirable electron-transport material in the photocatalytic process [20–23]. Therefore, a variety of graphene-based composites have been investigated, the majority of which are put forward for catalytic and electronic applications [7,23–25]. Some studies have been reported to composite graphene and Cu2O in the applications of lithium ion batteries, water splitting, biosensor and supercapacitors [26–29]. The application in photodegradation of organic pollutants was also studied while with a comparatively few number [30,31]. Moreover, there is no report on the elaborate and systematic analysis of synergistic effect between graphene and Cu2O particles in the photocatalysis process.

In this study, we report a mild one-pot in situ method to prepare octahedral Cu2O particles supported by reduced graphene oxide (RGO) at ambient temperature without surfactants. The as-synthesized octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites are characterized. The photocatalytic performances are evaluated and compared with pure octahedral Cu2O particles and RGO nanosheets by photodegradation of methyl orange (MO) under visible light. Scavengers are employed to further investigate the mechanism of photocatalysis process. The synergistic effect between RGO and octahedral Cu2O particles is discussed in detail.
All chemicals were analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

Highly purified natural graphite (99.99%, 500 mesh) was purchased from Qingdao Tianyuan Graphite Co., Ltd (Shandong, China). Phosphoric acid (H3PO4) (85%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (30%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), cupric acetate monohydrate (Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O), MO, sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4) (98%), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), triethanolamine (TEOA), tert butyl alcohol (TBA), and anhydrous ethanol were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl) (37%), hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Deionized (DI) water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was used for all experiments.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized by Hummers' method with some modifications and it has been illustrated by our previous work [32–34]. The synthesis of octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites was schematically displayed in Fig. 1 . Typically, a certain amount of as-synthesized GO sheets (10, 20, 25, 35 and 50 mg) were firstly dispersed in 50 mL DI water under ultrasonication for 10 min, resulting in a brown solution. Then 0.3 g Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O was added into the GO solution under vigorous magnetic stirring for 20 min to ensure that Cu2+ could be fully attached to the surface of GO sheets. The mixture turned grey. Subsequently, 10 mL 1 M NaOH was dripped into the solution after which the color of the solution turned dark blue. Finally 800 μL 20% N2H4·H2O was poured in the mixture and the solution was kept under stirring for 40 min. The solution turned dark brick red, declaring the generation of Cu2O. The as-prepared products were washed using DI water and absolute ethanol alternately, collected through centrifugation, and finally vacuum dried at 30 °C for 5 h. The products were named as Cu2O-RGO(10), Cu2O-RGO(20), Cu2O-RGO(25), Cu2O-RGO(35), and Cu2O-RGO(50), corresponding to the composites synthesized with 10, 20, 25, 35, and 50 mg as-prepared GO sheets, respectively. The pure octahedral Cu2O was prepared through a similar procedure, without adding GO sheets inside.

The field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Ultra Plus, Carl Zeiss, Germany) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) were employed to investigate the morphologies of the samples. X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker Corporation, Germany) was used to study the crystal structures and was scanned at rate of 6°/min from 5° to 80° by Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 Å) radiation. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded through a VERTEX 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Raman spectroscopy was investigated by NXR-FT-Raman module (USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by a Japan Kratos Axis UltraDLD spectrometer with a mono-chromated Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were analyzed by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained with Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer (USA). Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) measurements were carried out using a CHI760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China) in a three-electrode system with a 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) mixture as a redox probe in 0.5 M KCl solution. Samples acted as working electrodes and a platinum wire and a saturated calomel electrode served as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. EIS measurements were measured at open-circuit potential with 5 mV AC amplitude at a frequency range of 0.01 Hz–100 kHz.

The photocatalytic properties of the catalysts were tested by measuring the degradation of MO under visible light irradiation. Photocatalysts typically involving 20 mg RGO nanosheets, pure octahedral Cu2O particles, or octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites were dispersed in 45 mL aqueous solution of MO (10 mg/mL), where a 100 mL double-wall beaker with condensate water circulating cooling acted as the reaction vessel. The reaction system was maintained in the dark for 20 min under magnetic stirring to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium before the irradiation. Afterwards, a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a 420 nm cutoff filter (PLS-SXE300UV, Beijing PerfectLight Co., Ltd.) was employed as the light source. At given time intervals, the reaction solution (∼5 mL) was withdrawn and centrifuged immediately for the removal of the dispersed catalyst powders. The measurement of the absorbance intensity of MO solution at 464 nm was tested for the analysis of MO concentration.

The typical SEM images of obtained octahedral Cu2O particles and Cu2O-RGO composites were conducted to discuss their micro-structures and morphologies. Fig. S1 shows the SEM images of Cu2O-RGO composites synthesized with different amounts of GO sheets. It is observed that Cu2O-RGO(25) composites have the best composite structures with octahedral Cu2O particles homogeneously dispersed onto RGO sheets without agglomeration. Little or excess introductions of GO sheets lead to the stacking of Cu2O particles or weak combination between particles and nanosheets. In the follow-up studies, products named Cu2O-RGO(25) were used for research. Fig. 2 a and b exhibit the octahedral morphology of pure Cu2O particles with an average side length of around 700 nm. The side length of octahedral Cu2O particles fell to ∼400 nm after they were wrapped by RGO, as can be seen in Fig. 2c and d, indicating that the combination with RGO astricts the sizes of octahedral Cu2O particles. Theory has been proposed that the surface characteristics of GO play the role of anchoring sites to restrict the growth of Cu2O and help to disperse particles evenly on RGO sheets [35]. The smaller size of Cu2O particle contributes to more contacts with organic pollutant thus devotes more active sites to the photocatalysis process. The tight integration of Cu2O and RGO enables rapider electron transmission in the supporting RGO sheets, which thereby improves the photocatalytic activity of the Cu2O-RGO composites [36].

Fig. 3 shows TEM and HRTEM images of octahedral Cu2O particles and Cu2O-RGO composites. The typical TEM image of octahedral Cu2O is shown in Fig. 3a. Fig. 3b exhibits the fringe spacing of 0.27 nm which maps very specifically to that of the lattice space of {111} of the Cu2O crystals [37]. From Fig. 3c and d it is clear that the thin, transparent and crumpled RGO sheets cover on the uniform octahedral Cu2O particles and the fringe spacing can be identified as the lattice space of {111} of the Cu2O crystals as well, which is in coincidence with the SEM results shown in Fig. 2.

XRD technique was employed to analyze the crystal phase of pure octahedral Cu2O particles, octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites and GO sheets (Fig. 4 a and b). Fig. 4b is the enlarged region in Fig. 4a which is emphasized by blue dotted line to see the details. It is observed that the diffraction peaks of Cu2O all belong to planes of (110), (111), (200), (211), and (220), corresponding to the cubic Cu2O phase (JCPDS No. 65-3288) [38]. Meanwhile, the strongest peak is indexed to the (111) facet which is in accordance with HRTEM analysis. All the peaks of Cu2O can be found in the octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites as well. Fig. 4b shows that the peak intensity of (111) facet becomes weaker, narrower and blue-shifts in the octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites, simultaneously the peak centered at 26.6° appears, revealing that the growth of (111) facet was inhibited due to the existence of RGO [39]. Besides, no peak at 8.4° can be observed, further proves the great reduction of GO.

The transformation efficiency of the reduction of GO was further evaluated with FT-IR spectra and Raman spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum of GO is shown in Fig. 4c. The obvious absorption located at 3450 cm−1 can be regarded as OH bending vibrations. The peak at 1731 cm−1 and peaks at 1395 and 1065 cm−1 are attributed to the CO of carboxyl groups and characteristic CO stretching vibration, respectively [40]. The absorption at 1590 cm−1 results from sp
2 hybrids of graphitic domains in GO. The FT-IR spectrum of Cu2O-RGO composites exhibits reduced absorption intensity in the peaks that correspond to the oxygen groups (at 3450, 1731, 1395, 1220 and 1065 cm−1), indicating that most of the oxygen-containing functional groups are removed and GO has been converted to RGO successfully in Cu2O-RGO composites. The absorption at 648 cm−1 refers to the CuO stretching vibration in Cu2O phase, which is detected in the FT-IR spectra of both Cu2O and Cu2O-RGO composites, suggesting the effective combination of octahedral Cu2O and RGO. Raman spectra of GO and octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites show two characteristic bands: G band for sp
2 bonded carbons and D band for sp
3 defects in Fig. 4d [20]. After adding N2H4·H2O, the G band of the composites blueshifts from 1598 to 1585 cm−1 which possibly owes to the production of conjugated double bonds in RGO and defects caused by the growth of the Cu2O particles. Meanwhile, the intensity ratio of I
D
/I
G for the Cu2O-RGO composites is 1.33, which is much higher than that for GO (0.72), revealing the generation of tiny graphitic domains with edges serving on defects [41].

The reduction effectiveness of GO and Cu (II) via N2H4·H2O was further confirmed by XPS spectra in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a shows the whole spectrum of octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites which declares the existence of Cu2O and RGO. The XPS spectrum of Cu 2p in Fig. 5b shows sharp peaks at 932.2 and 952.3 eV which correspond to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 of Cu+, respectively [42]. The sharp peaks demonstrate that Cu2O is the only reduction product of Cu (II). Fig. 5c shows the broad C 1s peak in Cu2O-RGO, which can be deconvoluted into three peaks: CC(CC) at 284.7 eV, CO at 286.2 eV, and CO at 288.3 eV [43]. By integrating the area, the relative contents of graphitic carbon, CO, and CO are measured to be 65.92%, 14.51% and 19.57%, respectively. Whereas the C 1s XPS spectrum of GO in Fig. 5d shows deconvoluted peaks of them are reckoned to be 41.77%, 6.71%, and 51.52%, respectively. After reduction, a notable decrease in oxygen content can be clearly observed, indicating that GO has been dramatically reduced.

The photocatalytic performances of RGO, pure octahedral Cu2O, and all the Cu2O-RGO composites are evaluated by photodegradation of MO under visible light (λ > 420 nm). Fig. 6 a and the corresponding histogram in Fig. 6b show that Cu2O-RGO(20) and Cu2O-RGO(25) composites have better photocatalytic performances while MO dyes are 100% photo-decolored in 50 min. About 50.01% and 23.33% of the original MO dyes are degraded in the presence of octahedral Cu2O and RGO after 60 min, respectively. However, the photocatalytic efficiency of the as-prepared composites is largely inhibited when the introduction of GO sheets are excess. It may ascribe to the incomplete reduction of GO and bad integration between Cu2O particles and nanosheets. Fig. S2 shows the degradation of MO under illumination of visible light over different catalysts: Blank control, RGO, Cu2O, and Cu2O-RGO. With the visible light irradiation time increasing, the absorption peak situated at 464 nm steps down. Meanwhile, the first-order kinetics was introduced to evaluate the catalytic performance of the three catalysts. The apparent rate constants (k
app ) of different samples were measured from linear plots of –ln(C/C
0
) vs. time and are presented in Fig. 6c and d. It is clear that the as-prepared octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites demonstrate the highest reaction rate among the three catalysts with k
app = 82.88 × 10−3 min−1, while k
app = 8.88 × 10−3 min −1 for pure octahedral Cu2O and k
app = 3.61 × 10−3 min −1 for RGO. An explanation for the enhanced photocatalytic activity will be divulged below.

Meanwhile, comparisons of the present photocatalysis results of Cu2O-RGO with those of graphene based composites in the other literature are discussed. Graphene-based photocatalysts have been widely investigated as shown in Table 1 . It can be very clearly seen that our work owns the very remarkable photocatalysis ability among these graphene-based photocatalysts. Our octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites can degrade more MOs with fewer dosage and less time under visible light irradiation.

To further explain the principle of photocatalysis process, scavengers have been employed to study the effects of the reactive species such as O2 ¯, h+, and OH on the degradation of MO dyes [49–51]. Herein, PBQ, TEOA, and TBA were introduced as O2 ¯, h+, and OH scavengers, respectively [51]. 1 mM different scavengers were respectively added to the MO dyes in the presence of as-prepared Cu2O-RGO composites under same photocatalysis conditions. As shown in Fig. 7 , the introductions of TEOA and PBQ dramatically inhibit the degradation efficiency of MO with k
app decreasing a lot, indicating that h+ and O2 ¯ radicals play the main parts in photocatalytic degradation. On the contrary, the TBA scavenger demonstrates little effect on the photodegradation of MO dyes and its k
app increases to 98.93 × 10−3 min−1, which are ascribed to the reaction priority of OH radicals to NN double bond and phenyl ring in MO instead of TBA [52]. Also, photo-induced holes react with H2O to produce OH radicals. The introduction of TBA promotes the scavenging of holes and improves separation of the photo-induced electrons and holes thus the k
app raises [51]. In brief, h+ and O2 ¯ radicals are the main active species in the photocatalysis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic effect between RGO and octahedral Cu2O particles
ce:section.ce:para: Three primary steps are proposed during the photo-catalysis process: carrier generation, carrier separation and carrier transport on and across the catalyst-dye interface to propel the redox reactions. To examine the enhanced photocatalysis ability of the octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites, effective measurements are used and the results are discussed below.

UV–Vis spectra of pure octahedral Cu2O particles and octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites were compared in Fig. 8 a. The pure Cu2O particles exhibit a wide peak centered at ∼550 nm in visible light region. However, the Cu2O-RGO composites exhibit stronger and broader absorption spread over the whole visible region, suggesting that the as-prepared composites can harvest the sun's natural energy more effectively. According to the plots of (αhν)
2 ∼hν in Fig. 8b, the calculated band gaps of Cu2O and Cu2O-RGO are 2.10 eV and 1.94 eV, respectively. The narrower band gap of the composites means better collection of sunlight and easier generation of carriers.

PL spectroscopy are widely used to evaluate the separation efficiency of photo-induced electron/hole pairs and lifetimes of charge carriers [53]. The photo-induced electron/hole pairs recombine in semiconductors and bring about emitting of photons and heat, which is known as fluorescence [54]. There is a positive correlation between the fluorescence intensity and the recombination rate of electron/holes pairs. The PL spectra of pure octahedral Cu2O particles and Cu2O-RGO composites are displayed in Fig. 8c. It's obvious that compounding with RGO greatly decreases the fluorescence intensity compared to the pure Cu2O particles, declaring a longer carrier lifetime thus devotes more reactive species to decolor dyes. The as-prepared composites are proved to have enhanced photocatalytic activity.

EIS measurements have been performed to investigate the photo-induced charge transport on the catalysts [55,56]. As demonstrated in Fig. 8d, the Cu2O-RGO composites exhibit a smaller arc radius than the pure Cu2O particles on the EIS Nyquist plots, suggesting that the composites have lower electrochemical impedance. The inset is enlarged to show the solution resistances, where the Cu2O-RGO composites have a smaller one. Thereby, the RGO nanosheet accelerates the carriers with its wonderful electron delivery and promotes the high loading of photo-catalytic Cu2O particles. Meanwhile, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms are presented in Fig. S3. Specific surface areas for Cu2O and Cu2O-RGO obtained from the adsorption isotherms by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method are measured to be 9.01 and 8.49 m2/g, respectively. The two share very similar surface areas, indicating that the surface areas may not be the main factor to affect the photocatalytic activity in this system.

Overall, the synergistic effect between RGO and octahedral Cu2O particles demonstrated above endows the as-prepared octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites with outstanding photoelectric properties including strong and broad absorbance in visible light region, instant carrier separation, and low charge transfer resistance. The excellent photoelectric performances greatly enhance the photocatalysis ability of Cu2O-RGO composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic mechanisms of octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the discussions above, the photocatalytic mechanism about octahedral Cu2O-RGO semiconductor system has been proposed, as shown in Fig. 9 . The three main steps in the photodegradation process are concluded to be carrier generation, carrier separation and carrier transfer on and across the catalyst-dyes to promote the redox reactions which have been evaluated by UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 8a and b), PL spectra (Fig. 8c) and EIS results (Fig. 8d), respectively. When exposed to visible light irradiation, electrons are motivated from valence band to conduction band in Cu2O. The UV–Vis spectra have shown that the Cu2O-RGO composites exhibit a stronger and wider spectral response than pure Cu2O particles in the visible light region. Thus the carrier generation will be much easier. After the combination with RGO, the charge separation is significantly improved, which is proved by the PL spectra. The RGO nanosheets with superior conductivity here act as electron collector and transporter thus the carriers' lifetime is lengthened. As illustrated in Fig. 9, the photo-induced electrons in Cu2O will largely transport to the RGO nanosheets to react with dissolved oxygen molecules to form O2¯ radicals. Therefore the photo-induced holes will be separated effectively and react with OH¯ derived from H2O to obtain ·OH radicals. After which these radicals directly decolor MO molecules. The low charge transfer resistance verified by EIS measurement ensures fast carrier transport. Thereby the photocatalytic efficiency is significantly enhanced.

We have prepared octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites via a facile one-pot in situ method. The octahedral Cu2O particles in the composites exhibit an average edge length of around 400 nm and a good dispersion on the RGO nanosheets. The synergistic effect between RGO and octahedral Cu2O particles is analyzed systematacially for the first time. The synergistic effect endows the as-prepared octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites with outstanding photoelectric properties including strong and broad absorbance in visible light region, fast carrier separation, and low charge transfer resistance, leading to the enhanced photocatalysis performance in degradation of MO dyes. In addition, scavengers are employed to investigate the effects of reactive species such as O2 ¯, h+, and OH on the discoloration of MO dyes which turns out that h+ and O2 ¯ radicals are the main active species in the photocatalysis process. Overall, these results suggest that the octahedral Cu2O-RGO composites are promising materials in the development of solar energy and may be good alternatives to realize high-efficiency photocatalysts under visible light irradiation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial supports by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61671299, 51272159 and 81270209), Shanghai Science and Technology Grant (16JC1402000), the Program of Shanghai Academic/Technology Research Leader (15XD1525200), and the Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar) at Shanghai Institutions of Higher Learning (GZ2016005). We also acknowledge analysis support from the Instrumental Analysis Center of Shanghai Jiao Tong University and the Center for Advanced Electronic Materials and Devices of Shanghai Jiao Tong University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2017.04.095.
